description: A CEFR-aligned path from A1 to B1 — grammar, vocabulary, exercises, and reading for German that actually sticks.

levels.title: A1 — Beginner
levels.description: The building blocks: articles, present tense, and everyday vocabulary.

lessons.title: Der, Die, Das — Gendered Articles
lessons.summary: Why every German noun has a gender, and how to guess it.
content: Every German noun carries a grammatical gender: der (masculine), die (feminine), or das (neuter). This isn't about biological gender — der Löffel (the spoon) is masculine for no logical reason.

Some endings are reliable hints: nouns ending in -ung, -heit, -keit, or -schaft are almost always feminine (die Zeitung, die Freiheit). Nouns ending in -chen or -lein are always neuter, regardless of what they refer to (das Mädchen — 'the girl' — is neuter).
The best strategy is to learn every noun together with its article from day one, rather than memorizing nouns alone and guessing genders later.

lessons.title: Present Tense of Sein & Haben
lessons.summary: The two most irregular — and most essential — verbs in German.
content: Sein ('to be') and haben ('to have') are irregular but appear constantly, so they're worth memorizing before any other verb: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind.
Haben conjugates as: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. Both verbs also serve as helping verbs for other tenses later on, so fluency here pays off repeatedly.

lessons.title: Everyday Greetings
lessons.summary: Regional greetings from Guten Tag to Servus.
content: Guten Tag is the neutral, safe greeting anywhere in the German-speaking world. Moin is common in the north, Grüß Gott in Bavaria and Austria, and Servus in Austria and Bavaria as both hello and goodbye.
Wie geht's? ('How's it going?') is casual; the formal equivalent is Wie geht es Ihnen?, using the polite Sie form.

levels.title: A2 — Elementary
levels.description: Past tense, modal verbs, and vocabulary for daily independence.

lessons.title: The Perfekt Tense
lessons.summary: Talking about the past with haben/sein + past participle.
content: Most spoken-German past events use the Perfekt tense: a form of haben or sein plus a past participle placed at the end of the sentence — Ich habe einen Film gesehen ('I watched a movie').
Verbs of motion or change of state (gehen, fahren, aufstehen) use sein instead of haben: Ich bin nach Berlin gefahren ('I traveled to Berlin').

lessons.title: Modal Verbs: Können, Müssen, Wollen
lessons.summary: Ability, obligation, and desire, all with a shifted word order.
content: Modal verbs push the main verb to the infinitive at the end of the sentence: Ich kann gut kochen ('I can cook well'), Ich muss jetzt gehen ('I must go now').
Wollen expresses desire or intention: Ich will Deutsch lernen ('I want to learn German'). Note that wollen is stronger and more direct than möchten, which is the polite form of 'would like'.

lessons.title: Shopping & Prices
lessons.summary: Numbers, currency, and asking how much something costs.
content: Was kostet das? ('What does that cost?') is the essential shopping phrase. Prices in German are read with 'Euro' before the cents: 4,50 € is 'vier Euro fünfzig'.
Note that German uses a comma as the decimal separator and a period for thousands — the reverse of English convention.

levels.title: B1 — Intermediate
levels.description: Subordinate clauses, Konjunktiv II, and reading longer authentic texts.

lessons.title: Subordinate Clauses with Weil & Dass
lessons.summary: Sending the verb to the end — the hallmark of German subordinate clauses.
content: In a subordinate clause introduced by weil ('because') or dass ('that'), the conjugated verb moves to the very end: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin ('I'm staying home because I am sick').
This word order applies to a whole family of subordinating conjunctions — obwohl (although), wenn (if/when), and als (when, for a single past event) all trigger the same verb-final structure.

lessons.title: Konjunktiv II — Polite Requests & Hypotheticals
lessons.summary: Würde, hätte, wäre — softening statements and imagining alternatives.
content: Konjunktiv II expresses politeness, wishes, and hypothetical situations: Ich hätte gern einen Kaffee ('I would like a coffee') is far softer than the blunt Ich will einen Kaffee.

Wäre (would be) and hätte (would have) are the two irregular forms worth memorizing outright; for most other verbs, würde + infinitive covers the same function: Ich würde das nicht machen ('I wouldn't do that').

lessons.title: Reading a Short Newspaper Article
lessons.summary: Strategies for tackling authentic German news text.
content: German newspaper sentences are often long, with the verb pushed toward the end and multiple clauses nested together. Read for the main clause first — find the conjugated verb in second position — before untangling the subordinate clauses around it.
Compound nouns like der Bundesgerichtshof (the Federal Court of Justice) look intimidating but are simply smaller words glued together; breaking them apart usually reveals a meaning you already know.
